title: Elections for the government's chief executive
subtitle: Data by the Lexical Index of Electoral Democracy.
caption:
Elections for the government's chief executive

Data by the Lexical Index of Electoral Democracy.

The map displays the global status of elections for the government's chief executive in 2025, categorizing nations into three distinct groups based on the Lexical Index of Electoral Democracy. The visualization uses a color-coded scheme where blue indicates countries holding such elections, orange marks those that do not, and grey hatching denotes areas with no available data. The blue category, representing the presence of chief executive elections, covers the majority of the world's landmass and population centers. This includes the entirety of North and South America, most of Europe including the United Kingdom and France, and large portions of sub-Saharan Africa such as Nigeria and South Africa. Australia and India also fall within this blue band, indicating the existence of these electoral mechanisms. In contrast, the orange regions signify the absence of elections for the government's chief executive. This grouping is concentrated in the Middle East, encompassing Saudi Arabia, Iran, and the United Arab Emirates, as well as North Africa with countries like Algeria and Libya. In Asia, China and Vietnam appear in orange, alongside Myanmar. A smaller cluster of orange nations exists in Central Asia. Greenland stands out as the primary region marked with grey hatching, indicating that data is not available for this territory. The distribution shows a clear geographic divide, with the Americas and Western Europe almost entirely blue, while the Middle East and parts of East Asia are predominantly orange. The pattern visible on the map places the majority of countries in the category holding elections for their chief executive.
